demos.subject: Learn how to protect yourself against scams
demos.body:
Stay vigilant and report suspicious activity
Having trouble viewing this message?
View it online
instead!
Matthew, m
ost students don't think they will fall for a scam but unfortunately, many do.
Scammers are pretending to be UNSW and attempting to trick students into handing over money or personal details. Some UNSW students have lost thousands of dollars to scammers.
UNSW will never ask you to pay your tuition fees over the phone, on social media or by direct bank transfer.
Authentic emails from UNSW regarding your tuition fees will:
Direct you to pay fees on myUNSW and not through personal bank accounts
Come from an email address ending in @unsw.edu.au.
If you suspect a scam, there are steps you can take to protect yourself, your family and other students:
1. Keep yourself safe
Do not provide any information or money. Hang up any suspicious phone calls, delete suspicious emails and report suspected scams to
UNSW Protective Services
.
2. Report and get support
Contact UNSW Protective Services for guidance. If you have been targeted, please seek support from
Psychology and Wellness
or a
Student Support Advisor
.
Discover the
different types of scams
targeting UNSW students and how to spot them, and don't be afraid to seek help or advice.
Learn more about scams and what to do if you're targeted
If it doesn't feel right, report it
If you're unsure whether a message is from UNSW or a scammer, contact
The Nucleus: Student Hub
for confirmation.
For support or guidance on further actions, contact UNSW Protective Services.
Contact UNSW Protective Services
Back to top
^
Looking for help?
For advice on all things UNSW, including faculty specific questions, essential services, support and development, reach out to the Hub!
Contact The Nucleus: Student Hub
[NAME]

who are the Traditional Owners of the lands where each campus of UNSW is situated.
Copyright @2025 [NAME]. All rights reserved.

demos.subject: GENC[PHONE]_00696: Reminder: Learning Community reminder – There are 3 things you need so submit
demos.body:
GENC[PHONE]_00696
»
Forums
»
Announcements
»
Reminder: Learning Community reminder – There are 3 things you need so submit
Reminder: Learning Community reminder – There are 3 things you need so submit
by
Andrew Hingston
- Wednesday, 11 March 2026, 10:42 AM
The
Learning Community Assignment
is
due
1pm
this
Thursday 12 Mar 2026
(Week
4
)
.
There
are
three
things that you need to submit:
1. Post a summary (and discussion of the practical applications) on an article
under the closest forum topic under the ‘Learning Community’ section of the
course website.
2. Provide the details of the article under the ‘Learning Community Details’
link under the ‘Learning Community Submission’ section of the course website.
This includes author, title, URL etc.
3. Copy and paste the text of your forum post AND a screenshot of the post into
the Learning Community MS Word Template and submit it under the ‘Learning
Community Report’ link.
The post (1) is for the benefit of your fellow students. Providing the details
(2) allows us to check whether the article that you chose is unique. Copying
and pasting your post into the MS Word template and submitting it (3) is what
is used for both plagiarism detection and grading.
Note that only doing step 1 doesn’t constitute submission. You need to do all
three!
See this post in context

demos.subject: [COMP3331] Notice: Week 10: Final Week !!!
demos.body:
Wen Hu created a notice COMP3331 on Fri 08 November 2024 at 05:34:35 PM.
Week 10: Final Week !!!
Here are a few important announcements for Week 10, which will be the last teaching week of this term:
In the lectures, we will wrap up the Link layer, which we started last week and then move on to Wireless Network. The lecture slides are
available
.
Lab 5
will be due on 12/11/2024 (Tuesday) 10am.This week,
a tutorial
will replace the lab (in your usual labs). This will serve as useful practice for the final exam. Please look through the questions before hand and try to solve them by yourself. All questions on the tutorial are from past exams. So effectively, it is a sample exam.
Please complete the
myExperience surveys
. The response rate so far is very low (e.g., ~6%), and we would appreciate your feedback very much.
The
final exam
will be held
on campus
via
Inspera
(invigilated)
. We have made available several resources for the final exam, including a practice exam. The final exam will be held at:
COMP9331: 1:45pm to 4pm, 30/11/24 (Sat),
COMP3331: 9:45am to 12pm, 02/12/24 (Mon),
You can view and reply to the message by
clicking here.
Reply to this email to contact Wen Hu (
[EMAIL]
).

demos.subject: Learn how to protect yourself against scams 🔒
demos.body:
Stay vigilant and report suspicious activity
Having trouble viewing this message?
View it online
instead!
Matthew, do you know how to spot a scam?
Scammers can strike when you least expect them, tricking you into handing over money or personal details. Scammers are even pretending to be UNSW, resulting in some students losing thousands of dollars.
Take a minute to learn how to identify and respond to scams.
Recognise the red flags
🚩
It could be a scam if you're asked to pay your tuition fees:
Over the phone
On social media
By direct bank transfer
UNSW will never ask you to pay your fees via any method other than myUNSW.
Be aware that any person or service promising a discount on your fees is attempting to scam you and take your money.
Authentic emails from UNSW will:
Direct you to pay tuition fees on myUNSW  ✅
Come from an email address ending in @unsw.edu.au  ✅
Suspect a scam?
Take these steps to protect yourself, your family and other students.
1. Stop
Do not provide any information or money.
Hang up suspicious phone calls.
Do not click links in suspicious emails.
You can verify that a message has come from UNSW and not a scammer by contacting
The Nucleus: Student Hub
.
2. Report
Report suspected scams to
UNSW Protective Services
. Don't be afraid to report or seek advice. Speaking up protects you, your family and other students.
3. Get support
If you have been targeted, contact
Psychology and Wellness
or a
Student Support Advisor
.
Discover the types of scams that target UNSW students
Learn more about how to spot these scams, and what to do if you're targeted.
Learn more

demos.subject: Explore your career potential at UNSW
demos.body:
Enhance your potential at UNSW through personalised guidance from a dedicated career coach via our
Career Explorer Program
.
This is your opportunity to embark on a transformative roadmap to employability, expanding your possibilities and unlocking countless future opportunities in an evolving world.
The program consists of three in-person or online (your choice) coaching sessions:
Session One – group session (45 mins)
Session Two – individual session (30 mins)
Session Three – individual session (30 mins)
Please note
: if you have completed the Career Discovery Coaching Program you will not need to do the
Career Explorer Program
, as it is the same program with a new title.
Initial group session times are now available for Term 2.
Book initial session
Power up your career
We are delivering several practical
Career Seminars
at the beginning of Term Two, open to all UNSW students.
Workshop topics include Creating your resume, Job search strategies, Preparing for interviews, Networking and Recruitment assessments.
View session dates

SIG - graduate roles and internships

Visit UNSWConnect
to view thousands of other opportunities.

– an AHEGS-recognised,
UNSW Advantage
initiative that assists student mental health.
See more

Discover your employability strengths, identify skills that need development and earn 6UOC towards your degree via CDEV2000.

by

- Monday, 31 March 2025, 9:14 AM
Hi all, HW1 has now been graded and results are available on moodle. You can see a breakdown of your grade by clicking on your submission on moodle.
I've also released template solutions.
There is a review period (a link to the review form is available on moodle). If you have any grading issues, please fill out this form and I will get back to you. You should only fill this out after you have compared your ansewrs to the template, and you need to give a good reason for why you think your current grade is incorrect. The review period is open until this
Friday 4th April at 5pm.
Please do not email/contact us directly about grading issues - all such issues must go through the review process.
Thanks!
Omar

demos.subject: Announcement: an academic calendar for our future
demos.body:
Announcement: an academic calendar for our future
Wednesday 16 April 2025
Dear students
We’d like to thank the thousands of students who participated in our consultation process to evolve our academic calendar – including more than 5000 respondents to the all-student survey, as well as those who took part in advisory and focus groups.
In the final round of consultation, two calendar options were identified for consideration – an optimised trimester model and a new semester model with a hexamester (six-term) overlay.
Your insights have been incredibly valuable in helping us understand the characteristics of an evolved calendar that best meets your needs and the merits of both models. You also helped us decide which calendar will accommodate the future needs of our University and align with the ambitions of our new
UNSW Strategy: Progress for All
.
In 2028 the University will adopt a
semester calendar with the flexibility
to
deliver differentiated learning experiences to our diverse student cohorts.
This
calendar – a ‘flex-semester’ calendar – centres on delivering the best learning experiences and outcomes for all our students throughout their lifelong education journey.
The choice in calendar also ref [ORDER_ID] the overwhelming view of those who participated in our consultation process – to have a semester calendar with the flexibility to support the diverse needs of all our students.
The flex-semester calendar is a semester structure for our undergraduate and the majority of postgraduate students alongside an aligned hexamester (six-term) structure to support the growth of lifelong learning opportunities, including for our alumni throughout their careers, and more flexible postgraduate offerings.
The key features of
our new calendar include:
two standard 12-week teaching semesters
a dedicated mid-semester break, more aligned to
other major university and school holidays
optional s
ummer and winter terms for intensive or catch-up courses and accelerated study
alignment with UNSW’s existing hexamester calendar
(UNSW already offers some programs in a hexamester (six-term) model. The new calendar enables us to expand these offerings to suit specific student groups, typically postgraduate learners and professionals looking to upskill).
Our flex-semester calendar reimagines how we deliver education and acknowledges that you, our students, have diverse needs and learn in different ways. It will:
give students more time with a 12-week semester, especially our undergraduate students who have told us they want to engage more deeply with course material
provide flexibility and choice around study load, learning mode and balancing other life commitments such as paid work and extra-curricular activities, and access to new learning opportunities across the academic year
expand flexible learning options – online and in-person – for postgraduates and working professionals, ensuring greater access to education for all
allow academics and course designers to adapt in the future to accommodate the changing needs of our student cohorts.
When will this change happen?
We aim to introduce our new flex-semester calendar in 2028.
This timing means the majority of our current students will not be impacted by the calendar change. For those students whose program will straddle both the current calendar and the new flex-semester calendar, the University will work to ensure minimal disruption to your studies and will work with you to resolve any issues with your study load and facilitate the completion of your degree program on time.
Thank you again for your support and contributions to this important evolution of our Academic Calendar. We will continue to keep you updated as we finalise calendar details and move towards implementation in 2028.
For further information and FAQs visit our Academic Calendar
website
and you can direct any
enquiries to our
Student Hub
.
Best wishes
Professor Attila Brungs
Vice-Chancellor and President
Professor Sarah Maddison
Deputy Vice-Chancellor Education & Student Experience
Copyright © 2025 UNSW Sydney. All Rights Reserved.
Cricos Provider Code 00098G

demos.subject: Atlassian X DataSoc Datathon 2025
demos.body:
Calling all data, machine learning, and AI enthusiasts — as well as the creatives and innovators of tomorrow.
Have you ever wondered what it’s like to work as a Data Scientist or ML Engineer at Atlassian?
Introducing the
Atlassian X DataSoc Datathon 2025
— your opportunity to explore the world of Atlassian data science and solve real product challenges using data and AI.
What’s a Datathon?
Think hackathon, but focused on data. You’ll receive a rich, synthetic dataset modeled on Atlassian products (Jira, Confluence, Trello, Bitbucket), a real-world challenge, and a few days to uncover insights, develop solutions, and pitch your ideas to a panel
of judges. It’s all about creativity, teamwork, and clear communication.
Why You Should Join:
Work with a sandbox of Atlassian’s ecosystem: users, sessions, events, billing data, and more
Tackle challenges like predicting churn, modeling adoption paths, or building AI-driven recommendations
Build your portfolio with a real-world project
Network with Atlassian industry professionals
Compete for a personalised resume workshop with an Atlassian recruiter — plus other exciting prizes
Key Dates:
Final Presentations:
Tuesday, September 16th at the Atlassian Office
Sign-Up Deadline:
Thursday, 11th  September 2025
Sign
up here
before Thursday, 11th September 2025 — don’t miss out!
Facebook Event here
Best Regards,
CSE admin
Computer Science and Engineering,
Room 111D, Level 1, Building K17
UNSW SYDNEY 2052
E:[EMAIL]
T: [PHONE]
This email and any files transmitted with it may contain confidential information. If you believe you have received this email or any of its contents in error, please notify me immediately by return
email and destroy this email. Do not use, disseminate, forward, print or copy any contents of an email received in error.
